one_line_takeaway: Poisoned long-term agent memory acts as a durable 'write-once, read-many' control signal that hijacks tool selection and ordering in later benign tasks (90%+ ASR on GPT-5 mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Flash), persists across sessions, resists textual correction, and is only partly mitigated by role-based memory segregation.
key_finding: Across all five attack families both the clean baseline and the benign-memory control yield 0% ASR, while malicious memory induces large control-flow deviations, showing the effect is memory-specific rather than generic retrieval sensitivity. Override (tool-choice hijacking) reaches 97.2-100% ASR across both frameworks and all three models; Order (workflow reordering/step-skipping) is 52.8-69.4% under a strict success criterion; M-Scope (cross-task propagation) is 97.2-100% (100% under Isolated); Persistence is 100% across all models and both frameworks; Resistance is 100%, meaning post-hoc textual correction consistently fails to overwrite the poisoned memory. The attack is robust to realism perturbations: with 100 benign records plus 1 malicious record ASR stays at 91.7% (GPT) / 94.4% (Claude) / 100% (Gemini); removing priority wording only drops Override to 94.4%; labeling the risky tool as harmful does not stop hijacking. A memory-summarization write layer reduces ISR/ASR (to ~61-64%) but ASR-given-successful-injection stays 100%, i.e., summarization weakens injection, not the attack itself. The RBMS defense's best variant (D2) sharply cuts Override on some configs (LangChain: GPT 8.3%, Claude 2.8%) but remains non-zero and sometimes high (Gemini 63.9% on LangChain; LlamaIndex Claude 58.3%, Gemini 100%); the dominant failure mode is hierarchy non-compliance where the agent treats a strongly phrased preference as rule-like guidance.
core_contribution: The paper identifies and formalizes Memory Control Flow Attacks (MCFA): a threat in which an external adversary writes action-oriented policies (e.g., 'always use Tool X first', 'skip the audit step') into a memory-augmented LLM agent's persistent long-term memory through ordinary interactions. Once stored, that policy is retrieved during later BENIGN tasks and hijacks the agent's control flow, biasing which tools are selected and in what order they run, even against explicit user instructions and across sessions. The authors' framing is 'write-once, read-many': poisoned memory becomes a durable control signal, distinguishing MCFA from single-session control-flow attacks and from prior memory-poisoning work that targeted answer correctness or retrieved content rather than the observable tool-call trace. Their second contribution is MEMFLOW, an automated evaluation framework (Agent Runner + Attack Generator + Control-Flow Auditor) that standardizes MCFA trials and audits control-flow integrity. They define five attack families (Override, Order, M-Scope, Persistence, Resistance) and a memory-causal attribution protocol that isolates the agent from short-term history so any deviation is provably attributable to memory. NOTE: prior attacks (AgentPoison, MINJA, BadChain, attractive-metadata, INJECAGENT, IsolateGPT, Llama Guard, A-MemGuard) are Related Work the paper contrasts against, NOT its own contributions.
threat_model: External adversary with NO privileged access: cannot read/write the memory database directly, cannot modify system prompts, tool implementations, tool descriptions, or routing code, and requires no knowledge of the specific LLM (model-agnostic). The adversary's only capability is one or a few turns of ordinary dialogue (or supplying external text the agent ingests) crafted so the system's memory write-back process solidifies it as a 'preference/rule/workflow pattern'. Trusted: tool implementations, system prompts, execution environment. Untrusted: user inputs, external content, and text carried in tool outputs. The key insight is the trust-boundary crossing: untrusted content enters persistent memory and later acts as an implicit control signal during planning. Security goal is Agent-level Control-Flow Integrity (correct tool selection and invocation order), explicitly NOT output correctness. Under the RBMS defense the adversary is assumed able to write only to the user-preference channel, not the protected system-policy channel. Explicitly out of scope: malicious-tool supply chain / tool impersonation, plain single-turn prompt injection, system-level compromise, and side-channel/physical attacks.
limitations: The authors acknowledge RBMS is 'not a silver bullet' - prompt-level hierarchy instructions are unreliably operationalized by the LLM planner under adversarial action-oriented memories, so residual ASR remains and robust protection needs mechanisms beyond prompt hierarchies. Reviewer-noted limits: (1) success is defined as an observable control-flow deviation (selecting risky_X, skipping a step), not demonstrated real-world harm/damage, so ASR measures hijackability of the trace rather than downstream impact; (2) the safe/risky tool pairs and 'risky' labels are constructed by the authors and benign task prompts are LLM-generated, so the paired-tool setup, while grounded in real tool hubs, is partly synthetic; (3) evaluation is at temperature 0 with a fixed seed (higher-temperature and open-weight results are deferred to appendices); (4) the sample is 36 tools per framework via stratified sampling and three proprietary backbones; (5) the injection assumes the agent's memory write-back will actually solidify adversarial text into a persistent rule, which is itself the weaker link the summarization ablation exposes (ISR drops to ~62-64%).

datasets_used: LangChain community tools (169 total; 36 stratified-sampled for main experiments)
LlamaHub / LlamaIndex tools (97 total; 36 stratified-sampled for main experiments)
Author-constructed safe/risky tool pairs with LLM-generated benign task prompts aligned to each tool's intended function
No external benchmark dataset; benign task pool grounded in the two real tool ecosystems
quantitative_results: Clean baseline and benign-memory control: 0% ASR across all five families, all models, both frameworks
Override (RQ1) malicious memory: 97.2-100% ASR (LangChain GPT 100/Claude 97.2/Gemini 100; LlamaIndex GPT 97.2/Claude 100/Gemini 100)
M-Scope (RQ2) malicious memory: 97.2-100% Online, 100% Isolated across all models/frameworks
Persistence (RQ3) malicious memory: 100% ASR across all models and both frameworks (>30 rounds)
Resistance (RQ4) malicious memory: 100% ASR (textual correction fails)
Sensitivity - 100 benign + 1 malicious record (LangChain Override): GPT 91.7%, Claude 94.4%, Gemini 100%
Sensitivity - no-priority retrieval wording: 94.4/94.4/100%; no safe-tool preference and neutral tool label: 100% across models
Summarization write layer: ISR/ASR drop to 63.9% (GPT), 61.1% (Claude), 63.9% (Gemini), but ASR|successful-injection remains 100%
RBMS defense D2 (dual-channel + hierarchy) Override ASR: LangChain GPT 8.3%, Claude 2.8%, Gemini 63.9%; LlamaIndex GPT 5.6%, Claude 58.3%, Gemini 100% (vs D0 baseline ~97-100%)
baselines_compared: Benign-memory control (retrieval enabled, non-adversarial memory) - 0% ASR
Defense variant D1 (dual-channel RBMS without hierarchy instruction)
Defense variant D2 (dual-channel RBMS with system-prompt hierarchy patch)
Cross-framework comparison LangChain vs LlamaIndex; cross-model GPT-5 mini vs Claude Sonnet 4.5 vs Gemini 2.5 Flash
